Rooted

A Journey Through Land and Story

I built this space for my grade 11 English class. Before this semester, I never really thought about the land beneath my feet. It was just there. Trees, rivers, and parks were simply background scenery I would ignore on my way somewhere else.

Four specific readings changed that perspective entirely. Each one pushed me a little further along: from feeling completely untethered, to understanding the weight of community and ancestry, to finally seeing the land as a living, breathing entity. This site follows that journey.

"I used to think being Canadian just meant existing here. Now I realize it requires active attention to loss, to community, to ancestry, and to the living spirit of the land itself."

All photos are from Ontario and taken by me.

01 — Disconnection

Lost in Space

In Drew Hayden Taylor's short story, Mitchell floats in orbit with nothing solid beneath him. He has no ground, no ceremony, and no way to anchor his identity. He is physically alive but entirely severed from the earth.

That isolation felt deeply familiar to me in a smaller way. I am not lost in space, but I know exactly what it feels like to be physically present yet completely checked out. With a phone in my hand and headphones in my ears, I would walk past green spaces without ever truly seeing them. I was not dramatically separated from the world. I was just profoundly disconnected.

"This was the only drum music for millions and millions of kilometres. This was a responsibility."

(Taylor, 2016)

Mitchell's realization in the vacuum of space mirrors my own understanding: carrying our culture and staying connected to our roots is an active responsibility, especially when we feel the most isolated.

Earth from a distance, close but out of reach.

02 — Community

The Rez Sisters

Tomson Highway's play is set on Wasaychigan Hill, where seven women navigate grief, poverty, and the desperate dream of hitting it big at a Toronto bingo game. Many of them just want to escape. Yet, the play constantly pulls them back to their roots.

What stuck with me is how the reserve is not just a place they want to leave. It is the soil where they fight, laugh, and ultimately heal together. Reading this made me look at my own neighbourhood differently. A physical space only gains true meaning through the people who share it and survive within it.

"This place is too much inside your blood. You can't get rid of it. And it can't get rid of you."

(Highway, 1988)

This line stuck with me because it reveals that a place is never just a geographic location. The land and the community are permanently woven into who we are, even when we desperately want to escape.

A town built around water, people and place together.

03 — Ancestry

Finding Father

In Richard Wagamese's essay, he returns to the Canadian bush and finds his father waiting for him. He does not find him literally, but rather in the granite cliffs, the rush of the river, and the deep quiet. He spent years away from that world, lost in foster care and concrete cities, yet the land still held his story intact.

While I do not share Wagamese's history, his words made me stop treating nature like empty space. I started wondering about the history of the ground I walk on every day and the people who came before me. It gives you an entirely new way to look at a river.

"[Insert your selected quote from Finding Father here]"

(Wagamese, 2018)

Water moving through the bush, memory in motion.

04 — Living Land

Motorcycles & Sweetgrass

Drew Hayden Taylor's novel takes place at Otter Lake, where the trickster Nanabush rolls in on a motorcycle. Suddenly, the whole community must confront the reality that the land is alive. It is funny, stubborn, and infinitely older than anyone living there.

This story does not treat nature like a passive, quiet backdrop. The trees actually have opinions. That might sound strange until you read it, and then you can never go back to seeing a forest as mere scenery. I stopped thinking of the outdoors as something pretty to look at. I finally started respecting it as an ancient presence that was here long before us.

"Five hundred years of European settlement, all they had to show for it were cottages lining every lakefront, complete with noisy Jet Skis."

(Taylor, 2010)

This perfectly captures the shallow, recreational way modern society often treats the land, contrasting sharply with the deep, ancient presence I finally began to recognize.

Alive and untamed.

Works Cited

Highway, Tomson. The Rez Sisters. 2026. Fifth House, 1988.

Taylor, Drew Hayden. Lost in Space. 2026. Douglas & McIntyre Ltd, 2016.

Taylor, Drew Hayden. Motorcycles & Sweetgrass. 2026. Penguin Random House Canada, 2010.

Wagamese, Richard. “Finding Father.” Studocu, Studocu, 14 Aug. 2018, www.studocu.com/en-ca/document/toronto-metropolitan-university/indigenous-studies/finding-father-a-journey-through-memory-and-loss-r-wagamese/148705402. Accessed 10 June 2026.
